criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the three design principles of APPL: readability and flexibility, easy parallelization, and convenient conversion.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand APPL syntax and semantics, including APPL functions, expression statements, context managers, gen(), and the Definition class.
The plan includes the implementation of the @ppl decorator that marks APPL functions and creates an implicit prompt context.
The plan includes the implementation of expression statement capturing within APPL functions to append values to the prompt context via promptify.
The plan includes the implementation of the gen() function as an asynchronous LLM generation call that implicitly uses the accumulated prompt context.
The plan includes the implementation of the promptify mechanism for converting Python objects into string prompts appendable to the context.
The @ppl decorator has been implemented to mark APPL functions and provide an implicit PromptContext for each function.
The expression statement capturing has been implemented so that standalone expression statements within APPL functions are converted to prompts via promptify and appended to the prompt context.
The PromptContext class has been implemented to store prompt-related information (a scratchpad) as local and global prompts accessible via records() and convo().
The gen() function has been implemented to use the accumulated prompt in the APPL function context to call LLMs asynchronously, returning a StringFuture.
The promptify function has been implemented to convert Python objects (strings, function outputs, etc.) into prompt-compatible string representations.
The Role Changer context managers (e.g., AIRole, SystemMessage) have been implemented to specify the role/owner of messages in the chat prompt.

The Prompt Compositor context managers (e.g., NumberedList, DashList, StarList, LineSeparated, DoubleLineSeparated) have been implemented to control delimiter, indentation, indexing format, prologue, and epilogue of prompts.
The Definition class has been implemented as a special base class allowing custom definitions to be referred to in f-strings and instantiated with descriptions.
The output structure specification feature has been implemented in gen() allowing it to return structured Pydantic BaseModel objects via a response_model parameter.
The records() function has been implemented to retrieve the local prompt records from the current APPL function context.
The convo() function has been implemented to retrieve the full conversation (global prompts) from the APPL function context.
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the APPL runtime, including context passing, asynchronous semantics, tool calling, and tracing.
The plan includes the implementation of four inter-function context passing modes (new, copy, same, resume).
The plan includes the implementation of asynchronous LLM call semantics where gen() is non-blocking and synchronizes only when the result value is accessed.
The plan includes the implementation of automatic tool specification generation from Python function docstrings and signatures.
The 'new' context passing mode has been implemented to create a clean empty context for the callee APPL function (default mode).
The 'copy' context passing mode has been implemented to create a copy of the caller's context for the callee, so modifications do not affect the original (call-by-value semantics).
The 'same' context passing mode has been implemented so the callee shares the caller's context directly, with modifications applied to the original (call-by-reference semantics).
The 'resume' context passing mode has been implemented so the callee resumes its memorized context from the last run, making the function stateful.

The automatic tool specification generation (as_tool or equivalent) has been implemented to create OpenAI API-compatible JSON schema tool specifications from Python function docstrings (Google Style) and signatures.
The automatic execution of LLM tool call outputs as Python function calls has been implemented (e.g., via run_tool_calls()), returning tool results as observations.
The tracing module has been implemented to record APPL function calls and LLM request events (SendRequest, FinishRequest) for failure diagnosis and replay.
The LLM call caching mechanism has been implemented to cache responses and support failure recovery by loading cached results.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the compilation (transpilation) of APPL using Python's ast package with three key transformations.
The plan includes the implementation of the APPL compiler that transpiles APPL Python source using AST parsing and transformation to insert context management.
The APPL compiler has been implemented using Python's ast package to parse the original function as an AST, apply transformations, and compile back to a Python function.

The AST transformation for context management has been implemented, inserting the _ctx keyword parameter into APPL function definitions and propagating it through function calls annotated with __need_ctx__.
The AST transformation for capturing expression statements has been implemented, wrapping all standalone expression statements within APPL functions with an appl.execute() call.
The context propagation across nested APPL function calls has been implemented via appl.with_ctx() in the transpiled code.

The standalone f-string handling has been implemented in the compiler to split f-strings into sequential sub-expressions so that gen() calls within an f-string receive the prefix text before being invoked.
The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand StringFuture and BooleanFuture implementations for asynchronous execution.
The plan includes the implementation of StringFuture as a list-based representation of asynchronous string values that delays synchronization until str() is explicitly called.
The plan includes the implementation of BooleanFuture as an asynchronous boolean that enables future objects in control flows.
The StringFuture class has been implemented as a list of StringFutures with synchronization defined by Equation 1: str([s1,...,sn]) → str(s1) ⊕ str([s2,...,sn]).
The StringFuture concatenation has been implemented as defined in Equation 2: S ⊕ T = [s1,...,sn] ⊕ [t1,...,tm] = [s1,...,sn, t1,...,tm], enabling delayed concatenation of two StringFutures.

The StringFuture synchronization has been implemented so that str(S) waits and concatenates all constituent elements, and for unsupported methods (e.g., len()), it falls back to materializing S first.
The BooleanFuture class has been implemented to represent an asynchronous boolean that synchronizes only when bool() is called, enabling its use in control flow conditions.
The Future-based asynchronous execution runtime has been implemented using Python's concurrent.futures so that independent gen() calls run in parallel without blocking the main thread.
The agent has read Section 5.1 and Figure 7 completely to understand the CoT-SC case study comparison across APPL, LMQL, SGLang, and Guidance.
The plan includes the implementation of CoT-SC in APPL using a @ppl function that returns a list comprehension of parallel gen() calls.
The CoT-SC implementation has been coded in APPL as a @ppl function that returns [gen() for _ in range(num_trials)], achieving automatic parallelization as shown in Figure 7a.
The equivalent CoT-SC implementations in LMQL (with asyncio-based parallelization), SGLang (with lm.fork()), and Guidance (sequential) have been coded for comparison as shown in Figure 7.
The agent has read Table 2 completely to check the parallelization speedup results for CoT-SC, SoT, MemWalker, and ToT across different models.
The plan includes all four parallelization experiments: CoT-SC (10 branches), SoT (vicuna-80 top-20 instances), MemWalker (QuALITY first 20 articles), and ToT.
The CoT-SC parallelization experiment has been implemented with num_trials=10 branches, tested on gpt-3.5-turbo-1106 (OpenAI API) and a locally deployed LLAMA-7b on SGLang SRT backend.
The SoT (Skeleton-of-Thought) implementation has been coded in APPL using skeleton_prompt and point_expanding_prompt functions as shown in Figure 14, with parallel expansion of skeleton points.
The SoT experiment has been configured to use the top-20 data instances of the vicuna-80 dataset as in the original SoT paper.
The MemWalker hierarchical summarization has been implemented using the TreeNode dataclass with construct_leaf and construct_nonleaf APPL functions as shown in Figure 15.

The MemWalker experiment has been configured to use the first 20 articles from the QuALITY dataset, with 4000 characters per leaf node, 4 leaf nodes per intermediate node, and 2 intermediate nodes per top-level node (three-layer hierarchy).
The ToT (Tree of Thoughts) implementation has been coded in APPL with parallel execution of reasoning branches, tested on GPT-4o and LLAMA3.2-3B.
The speedup estimation has been implemented using Amdahl's law formula S = 1 / (sum_i pi/si) where pi is approximated by request count and si by the number of maximum parallelizable requests.
The parallelization experiments for all four tasks (CoT-SC, SoT, MemWalker, ToT) in both sequential and parallel modes have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: CoT-SC achieves approximately 9.49× speedup with GPT-3.5 and 9.34× with LLAMA-7b.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: SoT achieves approximately 2.79× speedup with GPT-3.5 and 3.17× with LLAMA-7b.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: MemWalker achieves approximately 3.07× speedup with GPT-3.5 and 1.84× with LLAMA-7b.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: ToT achieves approximately 9.39× speedup with GPT-4o and 6.48× with LLAMA3.2-3B.
The agent has read Section 5.3 and Figure 9 completely to understand the ReAct tool-use agent comparison between APPL and Guidance.

The plan includes the implementation of the ReAct agent with the three-stage loop: thoughts generation (tool_choice='none'), tool call actions generation (tool_choice='required'), and tool call execution for observations.

The ReAct agent has been implemented in APPL as shown in Figure 9a, including the iteration loop with AIRole context, thoughts generation via gen(tools=tools, tool_choice='none'), actions generation via gen(tools=tools, tool_choice='required'), and run_tool_calls().
The equivalent ReAct implementation in Guidance (Figure 9b) has been coded for comparison, with manual tool_names, tool_map, tool_desc specification and select() for structured generation.
The ReAct agent has been executed successfully without errors demonstrating tool calling functionality.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the AST-size metric comparing code conciseness across APPL, LMQL, and SGLang/Guidance for four tasks.
The plan includes the implementation of the AST-size metric using Python's ast.parse to count AST nodes as a measure of code conciseness.
The AST-size metric has been implemented using Python's ast package (ast.parse) to count the total number of AST nodes in programs written in different languages.
The AST-size comparison has been executed for CoT-SC, ReAct, SoT, and MemWalk across APPL, LMQL, and SGLang/Guidance implementations.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: APPL has smaller AST-size than LMQL and SGLang/Guidance across all tasks (CoT-SC: APPL=35, LMQL=57, SGLang=61; ReAct: APPL=61, SGLang=134; SoT: APPL=59, LMQL=120, SGLang=107; MemWalk: APPL=36, LMQL=64, SGLang=60).
The agent has read Section 5.5 and Appendix C completely to understand the LLM-based pairwise evaluation setup with four LLM judges across five criteria.

The plan includes the implementation of the LLM evaluation using GPT-4o, GPT-4 turbo, claude-35-sonnet, and DeepSeek-V2 as pairwise judges across succinctness, readability, intuitiveness, convenience, and overall criteria.
The Comparison Pydantic model has been implemented with fields: thoughts (str), succinctness, readability, intuitiveness, convenience, overall (each Literal['first', 'second', 'tie']).

The get_comparison() APPL function has been implemented as shown in Figure 16, using prompt compositors (StarList) to present evaluation criteria and gen(response_model=Comparison) for structured LLM output.

The pairwise comparison scoring has been implemented: the better code receives +1 point and the worse one -1 point, with final scores averaged across all LLM judges and normalized by number of comparisons.

The LLM evaluation has been executed with all four judges (GPT-4o, GPT-4 turbo, claude-35-sonnet, DeepSeek-V2) comparing APPL, Guidance, LMQL, and SGLang on the same code examples used in Section 5.4.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 8: APPL ranks the highest in almost all aspects (succinctness, readability, intuitiveness, convenience) and achieves the highest overall average score across all tasks.
The agent has read Appendix A.3 to understand how tool specifications are automatically generated from Python function signatures and Google Style docstrings.
The tool specification generation has been implemented to parse Google Style docstrings and function signatures, producing an OpenAI API-compatible JSON schema as shown in Figure 13.
The agent has read Appendix D (Figure 17) to understand the full transpilation result showing before/after APPL compilation of the question answering example.
The transpiled code correctly inserts PromptContext as a keyword-only parameter with default PromptContext() (_ctx: PromptContext=PromptContext()) in all APPL function definitions.
The transpiled code correctly decomposes standalone f-strings with embedded gen() calls into sequential sub-expressions using an enclosing Str() context manager.
The agent has read Appendix F.1 to understand the strict and non-strict reproducibility modes for tracing and failure recovery.

The strict reproducibility tracing has been implemented by annotating each generation request with a unique identifier and recording ordered SendRequest events and out-of-order FinishRequest events matched by identifier.
The non-strict reproducibility tracing has been implemented by grouping cached requests by generation parameters with a CommitRequest event, popping one cached result per cache hit from the group.

The trace visualization export has been implemented to support at least one frontend (Langfuse, Lunary, LangSmith, or Chrome Tracing) with hierarchical timeline information including start/end times, prompt, and cost.
The agent has read Appendix E to understand the multi-agent chat implementation using both explicit history management and resume context methods.

The multi-agent chat implementation using explicit history management has been coded (Figure 18a), where Agent._history is initialized via _setup(), retrieved and written in chat(), and updated with records() after each response.
The multi-agent chat implementation using resume context has been coded (Figure 18b), where the chat method uses @ppl(ctx='resume') to maintain stateful conversation history across calls.
The two-agent chat demo has been executed successfully showing two agents alternating conversation as shown in Figure 18c.
The agent has read Appendix G to understand how APPL implements a long prompt (1000+ tokens) from ToolEmu using BracketedDefinition classes and modular APPL functions.
The BracketedDefinition base class and concrete Definition subclasses (User, Agent, Simulator, UserInfo, etc.) have been implemented for reference-based prompt construction as shown in Appendix G.

The modular APPL functions for the ToolEmu agent prompt (global_defs, env_setup, tool_specification, agent_task_desc, agent_scratchpad, agent_format_instruction) have been implemented using prompt compositors for structured hierarchical formatting.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the feature comparison between APPL and existing prompt languages (LMQL, Guidance, SGLang).

The plan covers all key differentiating features of APPL from Table 1: automatic prompt capturing, automatic context passing, promptify tool, prompt compositor, Python-native generation output retrieval, generation without side-effects, and failure recovery.
The generation-without-side-effects feature has been implemented in gen(), allowing LLM generation calls that do not write results back to the prompt context when called without assignment.
The agent has read Appendix B.1 to understand the Amdahl's law-based speedup estimation methodology used in Table 2.
The local SRT benchmark experiments have been configured to run on an NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU with 24 GiB memory using SGLang's SRT backend for LLAMA-7b.
The execution outcomes confirm that actual speedup ratios in Table 2 approximately match the estimated speedups derived from Amdahl's law across the four parallelization experiments.
